questions.question: What is the correct order of Tuckman's five stages of team development?
options: Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, Adjourning
Forming, Norming, Storming, Performing, Adjourning
Storming, Forming, Norming, Adjourning, Performing
Norming, Forming, Storming, Performing, Closing
questions.explanation: Tuckman's stages are Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, and Adjourning. Storming — the conflict-heavy stage — is a necessary step on the way to Performing.

questions.question: Who coined the modern concept of "psychological safety"?
options: Amy Edmondson (1999)
Bruce Tuckman (1965)
Patrick Lencioni (2002)
Robert Greenleaf (1970)
questions.explanation: Amy Edmondson introduced psychological safety in 1999 — the shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. Tuckman authored the stages model; Lencioni the five dysfunctions; Greenleaf servant leadership.

questions.question: What did Google's Project Aristotle find to be the single most important factor in effective teams?
options: Psychological safety
Hiring the most senior engineers
Strict deadlines
Individual star performers
questions.explanation: Project Aristotle found psychological safety to be the top factor, ahead of Dependability, Structure & clarity, Meaning, and Impact. Team composition (e.g., star players) mattered far less than how the team worked together.

questions.question: Per the 2020 Scrum Guide, how many people should a Scrum Team typically have?
options: 10 or fewer
Exactly 7
As many as the work requires, with no guidance
questions.explanation: The 2020 Guide says typically 10 or fewer people — small enough to stay nimble, large enough to do significant work. If bigger, split into multiple teams sharing one Product Goal and backlog.

questions.question: On a healthy team, a consistently silent retrospective with no dissent usually signals strong agreement.
questions.explanation: The absence of dissent is usually a tell for low psychological safety (suppressed concerns), not genuine alignment. Unanimous agreement is often fear, not consensus.

questions.question: In Lencioni's Five Dysfunctions of a Team, what is the root dysfunction and how should the pyramid be fixed?
options: Absence of (vulnerability-based) Trust, fixed bottom-up starting with trust
Inattention to Results, fixed top-down starting with results
Fear of Conflict, fixed by enforcing harmony
Avoidance of Accountability, fixed by adding managers
questions.explanation: Absence of Trust (vulnerability-based) is the root. You fix bottom-up: build trust, then make conflict safe, then drive commitment, accountability, and results. Vulnerability-based trust ≈ psychological safety.

questions.question: According to Edmondson, how does psychological safety relate to high standards?
options: Safety plus high standards is the high-performance zone; safety plus low standards is a comfortable, coasting team
Safety means lowering standards so no one feels stressed
Safety and standards are unrelated
High standards require removing psychological safety
questions.explanation: Safety is not niceness — it's what makes hard feedback possible. Edmondson pairs it with high standards: high safety + high standards is high performance (and aligns with Radical Candor: care personally, challenge directly).

questions.question: A team has polite meetings, no visible conflict, and keeps missing its goals. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Artificial harmony — Fear of Conflict masking an Absence of Trust, leading to weak commitment and missed results
A healthy high-performing team that simply had bad luck
Too much conflict that needs to be suppressed further
Excessive psychological safety lowering standards
questions.explanation: Polite silence with missed goals is classic artificial harmony: suppressed dissent (Fear of Conflict over an Absence of Trust) yields weak buy-in (Lack of Commitment) that quietly slips (no Accountability), hence poor Results.

questions.question: How many two-way communication channels exist among n people?
options: n²
2n
questions.explanation: The number of pairwise channels is n(n−1)/2 — it grows quadratically, which is part of why smaller teams communicate more easily and large teams need to split.

questions.question: What does Brooks's Law state?
options: Adding people to a late software project makes it later
Doubling the team always doubles the throughput
Smaller teams always ship faster than larger ones
Communication overhead is constant regardless of team size
questions.explanation: Brooks's Law: adding manpower to a late software project makes it later — onboarding cost and the quadratic growth in communication channels outweigh the added hands in the short term.

questions.question: The 2020 Scrum Guide changed the team's autonomy term from "self-organizing" to "self-managing."
questions.explanation: 2020 changed "self-organizing" (who/how) to the broader "self-managing" — the team internally decides who does the work, what to work on, and how.

questions.question: A team has been stuck in Storming for weeks. What is the best Scrum Master response?
options: Diagnose why, then make conflict safe and explicit (mine for conflict), tighten working agreements, and clarify roles/goals — do not suppress it
Restore surface calm by discouraging any disagreement
Escalate the whole team to management for a reset
Wait it out — Storming always resolves itself with time
questions.explanation: Stuck-Storming usually means conflict is avoided or has become personal. Surface it in a facilitated session, separate people from ideas, tighten norms, and clarify roles/goals. Suppressing the conflict only defers it.

questions.question: What does Conway's Law assert?
options: A system's design tends to mirror the communication structure of the organization that builds it
Teams should be organized strictly by technical layer
Adding architects always improves system quality
System complexity is independent of team structure
questions.explanation: Conway's Law: organizations design systems that mirror their own communication structures. It's why misaligned team boundaries produce integration friction, and why aligning teams to value streams can remove dependencies.

questions.question: How do Tuckman, Lencioni, and Edmondson/Aristotle complement each other as diagnostic lenses?
options: Tuckman locates the stage, Lencioni diagnoses what's broken (and in what order to fix), Aristotle/Edmondson says what to invest in (safety as foundation)
They are three names for the same five-stage model
Tuckman fixes results, Lencioni fixes safety, Aristotle fixes conflict
Only one can apply to a given team at a time
questions.explanation: Tuckman to locate the developmental stage, Lencioni to diagnose what's broken and the bottom-up fix order, Aristotle/Edmondson to decide what to build — with psychological safety as the foundation.

questions.question: What is the biggest pitfall of hybrid/remote work for team fairness?
options: Proximity bias — in-office people are heard and promoted more than equally capable remote peers
Remote workers always outperform in-office workers
Hybrid work eliminates all communication overhead
Co-located teams can never be psychologically safe
questions.explanation: Proximity bias means physically present people get disproportionate visibility, airtime, and advancement. Countering it (equalizing airtime, remote-first practices) is key to fairness on hybrid teams.

questions.question: Which of the following are among Project Aristotle's five keys to team effectiveness? (Select all that apply.)
options: Psychological safety
Dependability
Structure & clarity
Individual seniority
questions.explanation: The five keys are Psychological safety, Dependability, Structure & clarity, Meaning, and Impact. Individual seniority/star talent was notably NOT among the top drivers.

questions.question: What do the "two-pizza rule" and Dunbar's number refer to, respectively?
options: A team of roughly 5–8 people; ~150 stable relationships (which bounds orgs, not single Scrum teams)
A team of ~20 people; ~50 stable relationships
Both refer to the same ideal Scrum team size of 7
A budget rule; a velocity ceiling
questions.explanation: The two-pizza rule keeps a team small enough to feed with two pizzas (~5–8). Dunbar's number (~150) is the cognitive limit on stable relationships — it bounds organizations, not individual Scrum teams.

questions.question: What is the fastest single diagnostic of team psychological safety?
options: How fast and freely bad news travels to you
How many story points the team completes
Whether meetings always end early
How rarely anyone disagrees with the leader
questions.explanation: Watch whether bad news travels fast and freely — on a safe team, problems and dissent surface early. Rare disagreement is a warning sign (fear), not a strength.
